costs: Large-scale infrastructure and staffing transition costs analogous to Poland's ~7,000 school closures and ~10,000 potential teacher job losses
Negotiation costs with autonomous church/private maintainers outside Klebelsberg Centre authority
Transition-cohort disruption risk (double cohorts, curriculum realignment)
content.equity_impact: Likely positive: delaying selection is the most directly evidenced lever against the inequality mechanism (F-live-international_comparison-02, F-live-polish_reform-04), though effect on mean performance is contested (F-live-international_comparison-07) and durability is uncertain given Poland's reversal.
expected_benefits: Reduced inequality of opportunity consistent with cross-national tracking-delay evidence
Potential PISA and labour-market gains analogous to Poland's 1999-2016 experience
Reduced elite self-separation effect flagged by the Education Minister as not attributable to institutional value-added
content.goal: Raise the age of first academic selection from 10 to at least 14-16, reducing the number of pre-adolescent sorting points and associated inequality of opportunity.

implementation_steps.action: Draft legislative amendment to Act CXC of 2011 phasing entry point from grade 5/7 to grade 9, with transition cohort protections
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education, in negotiation with Klebelsberg Centre and non-state maintainers
implementation_steps.timeline: Years 1-2 (legislative drafting and negotiation with church maintainers per F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

implementation_steps.action: Enact phased statutory change with multi-year implementation runway to avoid Polish-style double-cohort shock
Convert or merge existing 6-/8-year track infrastructure into extended comprehensive lower-secondary provision
implementation_steps.timeline: Years 3-8, phased by cohort to avoid abrupt closures

mechanisms: Later selection reduces the number of school-type sorting points, and international comparative literature associates earlier selection age and more school-types with higher inequality of opportunity
Poland's 1999 reform delaying tracking to 16 produced improved PISA outcomes and labour-market gains (3pp employment, 4% earnings) via comprehensive middle-tier restructuring
Removing early selection reduces the 'brain drain' externality on ordinary schools' peer composition, though this specific effect remains unquantified
risks: Institutional durability risk: Poland's reform was substantially reversed after 2016 despite measured success, suggesting reform could be politically reversed
High symbolic salience already demonstrated in Hungary, where even non-abolition proposals were reframed as 'communist' unification, indicating strong opposition mobilization capacity
Teacher workforce (half aged 50+, only 7.2% under 30, low relative pay) may lack capacity for implementation fidelity
content.title: Delay selection age to 14-16 (Polish/Finnish-style comprehensive extension)
